content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
. Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
. Fax (916) 322-0886
April 30, 1999
Mr. Michael Miller
City of Arcadia
Office ofthe City Attorney
240 West Huntington Drive
Post Office Box 60021
Arcadia, California 91066-6021
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-019(a)
Dear Mr. Miller:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of council member Gino
Roncelli regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
QUESTIONS
You are requesting reconsideration of our recent advice letter to you, No. A-99-019. That
letter concluded that if council member Roncelli has received over $250 in income from the Los
Angeles Turf Club in the past 12 months, from owning horses that race at Santa Anita, he must
disqualify himself from participating in decisions about the development ofportions of Santa
Anita Park that would have a material financial effect on the Los Angeles Turf Club, or on any
business entity that is a parent, subsidiary, or is “otherwise related” to the Turf Club.
a. You reiterate your argument that the Los Angeles Turf Club is not a source of income
to council member Roncelli.
b. You argue that state law precludes the application of conflict of interest provisions to
race horse owners, relying on Business and Professions Code section 19424.5, which provides
that members of the California Horse Racing Board are not disqualified from making decisions
about the horse racing industry even though they have received income from horse racing.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-019(a)
Page 2
c. You raise First Amendment and policy arguments as to why the Act’s conflicts of
interest laws should not prohibit Mr. Roncelli from participating in decisions about the
development ofproperty at the Santa Anita race track.
CONCLUSIONS
a. Despite the additional information and arguments you set forth, we stand by the
conclusion in our original advice letter that, for purposes ofthe Act, the Los Angeles Turf Club
is considered a source of income to council member Roncelli. We recognize that state law
governs horse racing and prescribes the method for determining the amounts paid out of the
purse to winning race horse owners, as you have described in your requests for advice. For
purposes of the Act’s conflict of interest laws, however, we do not find any precedence or
rationale for ignoring the Los Angeles Turf Club as a legal entity, and looking through it to find
that the individual bettors are sources of income to council member Roncelli instead ofthe
corporation.
b. Regulation 18707.4 of the Political Reform Act permits a narrow exception to the
conflict ofinterest rules for industry representatives on regulatory boards. Business and
Professions Code section 19424.5 was adopted pursuant to this regulation, and only applies to
members ofthe California Horse Racing Board. It does not apply to local council members like
Mr. Roncelli.
c. The financial disclosure and conflict ofinterest rules ofthe Political Reform Act have
been upheld in numerous cases. The Act’s policy goals of unbiased decision-making support
council member Ronceffi’s disqualification from a decision affecting an entity that is a source of
income to him.
FACTS
2
Mr. Gino Roncelli was elected to the Arcadia City Council in April of 1998. Arcadia is
the home of the Santa Anita Park, a horse racing site and adjacent parking lot area. The total site
which comprises approximately 317 acres is owned by the Magna International, Inc. (“Magna”),
a Canadian auto parts maker. It is anticipated that the development of the property will be sought
by Magna Development. Magna Development, a subsidiary ofMagna, plans to seek
development ofpart of Santa Anita Park that is now used as a parking lot. Continuing down the
corporate structure, the Santa Anita Companies are a subsidiary of Magna Development and the
Los Angeles Turf Club is a subsidiary corporation ofthe Santa Anita Companies. The Los
2 The facts provided in your initial request for advice and your request for reconsideration are summarized
here.


File No. A-99-019(a)
Page 3
Angeles Turf Club is the operator ofthe racetrack and functions as the racing association as
described below. The Turf Club does not function in the arena of land development.
Magna is contemplating development ofpart of Santa Anita’s parking lot area into
various commercial uses. This will entail the processing of a development application before the
city which will include rezoning and related discretionary legislative type decisions by the city
council after review by the city planning commission. This will also involve environmental
review by the city council. The financial effect of these decisions on Magna is material. These
decisions do not involve the racetrack (Turf Club) or horse racing. They involve separate uses
that will most likely include theaters, restaurants, commercial and residential. Parking for horse
racing will still be in accordance with city code and parking requirements.
Prior to his election as a city council member, Mr. Roncelli owned race horses which, on
occasion, participated in the racing program at Santa Anita. His horses also race at other race
track sites in California. He currently has an ownership interest in some horses that may, within
twelve months of any city decisions, derive revenue from racing at the Santa Anita Park in
excess of $250.
Santa Anita Park is a state licensed site for race horse owners to enter their horses into
competition. The Turf Club has no direct relationship with the horse owners, including Mr.
Roncelli. They operate the horse racing venue subject to complete regulation by the State Horse
Racing Board. You have provided the following description ofhorse racing in California.
Horse Racing. The applicable statutory law for horse racing is set forth in the State
Business and Professions Code commencing at section 19400. Under the system known as pan
mutual wagering, the racing association acts as a stakeholder for all wagers, deducting from each
pan-mutual wagering pool the statutory takeout, which includes the state license fees, the
percentage deduction for purses, and the race track’s revenue percentage. The racing association
for the Santa Anita Park is the Los Angeles Turf Club.
Purses constitute what is paid to the horse owner. The purses for race meetings are
determined by established rate schedules negotiated in advance of a racing meet by the
Thoroughbred Owners of California and the Turf Club. Pursuant to state law, race tracks in
California are accorded a slot ofthe calendar to conduct competition at their location. The Turf
Club operates Santa Anita Park for this purpose during the December - April time period. This
time slot is also referred to as the racing meet.
The actual purses to be paid for any one race, or for the day’s races, initially are
determined by the racing secretary, an employee of the Turf Club, based on projections of the
handle (amount wagered). The purse is often revised via negotiations between the Thoroughbred
Owners of California and the Turf Club during the course of the race meeting based on the actual
handle. The handle which is the amount bet, comes from both on track betting and off-track
betting.


File No. A-99-019(a)
Page 4
The amount of the total purse is determined through negotiations between the TOC and
the Turf Club prior to the race meet. Usually, revenue is paid to those owners whose horses
finish one through six in any given race. This revenue is a percentage established by statute. Of
course, the races are competitive and the results are a matter of speculation.
In sum, a race horse owner may derive revenue ifhis or her horse finishes within a
required place in a race. If the owner is entitled to revenues, their source is the handle which is
the money bet by members of the public who attend the racing event at Santa Anita Park and at
off-track betting facilities. The amount of the purse and its percentage allocation is pre
determined before the racing meet based on the prospective handle. Similarly, the handle is the
source of income to the Turf Club.
ANALYSIS
a. Source of Income. The analysis with respect to whether the Los Angeles Turf Club is
considered a source of income to council member Roncelli under the Act is set forth in our initial
advice letter to you, No. A-99-019, and summarized here.
The purpose of the Act’s conflict of interest provisions is to ensure that government
officials make decisions unbiased by their own financial interests. (Section 8 1001(b).) In this
case, the decisions facing Mr. Roncelli are upcoming Arcadia City Council decisions about
Magna’s proposal to develop part ofthe parking lot at Santa Anita Park into theaters, restaurants,
and other commercial and residential uses.
Section 87100 ofthe Act prohibits a public official, such as council member Roncelli,
from making a governmental decision in which he has afinancial interest. Section 87103 ofthe
Act states that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official or on any ofthe official’s economic interests.
Section 87103 sets forth the official’s economic interests in five categories: (a) a
business entity in which the official has an investment of $1,000 or more; (b) real property in
which the official has an investment of $1,000 or more; (c) any individual, business or entity that
has been a source ofincome to the official of $250 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision; (d) a business entity that the official manages or is an employee of; and (e) an
individual or entity that has made gifts to the official of $250 or more within 12 months prior to
the decision. (Section 87103 (a)-(e).)
In council member Roncelli’s case, the economic interest at issue is a source of income to
him. Section 87103(c) states that an official’s economic interests include:


File No. A-99-019(a)
Page 5
“Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course ofbusiness on
terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.”
In this case, council member Roncelli has received over $250 from the Los Angeles Turf
Club in the past. He also has an ownership interest in some horses that may, within 12 months of
upcoming city council decisions, derive revenue from racing at Santa Anita Park equal to or in
excess of $250. As discussed in the previous advice letter, it is not disputed that when Mr.
Roncelli receives prize money from a racing meet, those funds are accurately characterized as
income under section 82030. However, you argue that the source of income to Mr. Roncelli is
not the Los Angeles Turf Club, but rather the individual bettors.
In our initial advice letter, we concluded that for purposes ofthe Act, the Los Angeles
Turf Club is considered the source of income to council member Roncelli. The analysis
supporting this conclusion is fully set forth at pages 5-7 of that letter. In summary, we found that
despite the fact that the funds the Turf Club distributes come from the money bet by the public,
the Turf Club is the corporate entity that is the source of income to Mr. Roncelli.
The question of whether the Turf Club is a source of income to council member Roncelli
is rather straightforward. The checks Mr. Roncelli receives from racing horses at Santa Anita are
issued by the Los Angeles Turf Club. Mr. Roncelli reports income from the Los Angeles Turf
Club, not from individual bettors, on his income tax returns. And the entity that is legally liable
for paying owners ofwinning horses at Santa Anita isthe Los Angeles Turf Club, not the
individual bettors. It is the Los Angeles Turf Club to whom Mr. Roncelli would have legal
recourse if they failed to pay him for a winning race or paid him the wrong amount. We
recognize that the source ofthe funds the Los Angeles Turf Club distributes comes from the
betting public and that the method for distributing funds to horse owners is prescribed by statute,
which requires that the Turf Club and the Thoroughbred Owners of California negotiate the
amount ofthe purse paid to winning race horse owners. However, just because an official
receives a payment from a corporation, at a rate or amount that is set by statute, does not
necessarily mean that the corporation making the payment is not considered a source of income
to the official under the Act.
As we discussed in our previous advice letter, in applying the Act’s conflicts rules, the
norm is to respect the corporate form. It is only in unusual circumstances where the Commission
or previous advice has “pierced” through entities, such as corporations, which are the apparent
source of income, to treat the controlling person as an additional source of income or economic
interest of the official’s. (See e.g., Lahr Advice Letter, No. 1-98-298; Brown Advice Letter, A-
97-122; Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-80-03-069; In re Nord (1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6). In such
cases, the advice looked through an entity to find that an additional individual or entity should


File NoA-99-019(a)
Page 6
be considered an economic interest of the officials.
In this case, you argue for looking through
the Los Angeles Turf Club to find, in effect, no economic interest ofthe council member’s,
because no individual bettor will be a source of $250 or more in income to him.
The Los Angeles Turf Club is more than an “intermediary” or “pass-through” for funds.
As stated in the facts you provided, the purses for race meetings, i.e., what is paid to winning
horse owners, are determined by rate schedules negotiated in advance of a racing meet by the
Thoroughbred Owners of California and the Los Angeles Turf Club. So the Turf Club is an
active participant in negotiating the amount that will be paid to winning horse owners.
In your request for reconsideration, you make the additional argument that “house
gambling” is illegal in California and that by “converting Santa Anita into a source of income
with regard to monies bet by thó public and paid out by statute to horse owners via the purse, the
FPPC is, in effect, analogizing Santa Anita to the ‘house’ with regard to gambling....” We do not
agree that finding the Los Angeles Turf Club as the source of income to council member
Roncelli for purposes of the Act’s conflict of interest laws will convert the system of legalized
gambling in California into a system ofhouse gambling, as you argued. The Zweben Advice
Letter, No. 1-94-3 80, concluded that a local official could not participate in the cardroom
approval process ifhe had received $250 or more in horse race winnings from the racetrack that
was requesting a license for a cardroom gaming facility. The Zweben advice letter is consistent
with our conclusion that the Los Angeles Turf Club is a source of income to council member
Roncelli, and has had no effect on the status of gambling in California.
b. Industry Representatives on Regulatory Boards. Next you argue that because
members of the California Horse Racing Board are permitted to participate in decisions affecting
the industry even though they own race horses and receive purses, council member Roncelli
should be able to as well. Shortly after the Political Reform Act was adopted in 1974, the
question arose whether the Act’s conflict of interest rules should prohibit industry members of
state regulatory boards from making decisions affecting the regulated industry. In 1976, the
Commission adopted regulation 18703, the predecessor of current regulation 18707.4 (previously
numbered 18703.3), in order to interpret the Political Reform Act harmoniously with the
numerous state statutes providing for industry representation on regulatory boards. Regulation
18707.4 creates a narrow exception from disqualification under the Act’s conflict of interest laws
for members ofboards or commissions who, by law, are required to have a particular economic
interest to be on the board. Regulation 18707.4 provides:
“18707.4. Public Generally: Appointed Members of Boards
and Commissions
(a) For the purposes of Government Code section 87103, the
‘public generally’ exception applies to appointed members of
boards and commissions who are appointed to represent a specific
economic interest, as specified in section 87103(a) through (d), if
all of the following apply:


File No. A-99-019(a)
Page 7
(1) The statute, ordinance, or other provision of law which
creates or authorizes the creation of the board or commission
contains a finding and declaration that the persons appointed to the
board or commission are appointed to represent and further the
interests of the specific economic interest.
(2) The member is required to have the economic interest the
member represents.
(3) The board’s or commission’s decision does not have a
material financial effect on any other economic interest held by the
member, other than the economic interest the member was
appointed to represent.
(4) The decision ofthe board or commission will financially
affect the member’s economic interest in a manner that is
substantially the same or proportionately the same as the decision
will financially affect a significant segment of the persons the
member was appointed to represent.
(b) In the absence of an express finding and declaration of the
type described in Title 2, California Code ofRegulations, section
18707.4(b)(l), the ‘public generally’ exception only applies if such
a finding and declaration is implicit, taking into account the
language ofthe statute, ordinance, or other provision of law
creating or authorizing the creation ofthe board or commission, the
nature and purposes ofthe program, any applicable legislative
history, and any other relevant circumstance.”
The Commission discussed the history ofthe exception for industry representatives on
regulatory boards in the Callanan Opinion:
“The subsections were adopted in an effort to reconcile the
conflict ofinterest provisions ofthe Political Reform Act with
other statutes which require certain boards to include as members
persons who represent the industry, trade or profession which the
board oversees. The Commission was persuaded that when the
legislative body which creates a regulatory board determines that
industry views and expertise should be represented on the board,
the Political Reform Act should not be interpreted to prevent
industry members from participating in board decisions affecting
the industry.” (In re Callanan, 4 FPPC Ops. 33 (1978), p. 4.)
The Callanan Opinion dealt with the State Board ofFuneral Directors and Embalmers, an
eight-member board, ofwhom three members were required to be licensed funeral directors or
embalmers, and the remaining five were required to be public members. The Commission


File No. A-99-019(a)
Page 8
applied regulation l8703, the predecessor to current regulation 18707.4. The Commission
concluded that the three industry members ofthe Funeral Board were not required to disqualify
themselves from a decision about requiring the consent ofnext ofkin before embalming a body,
which would have required many in the industry to purchase refrigeration equipment.
After the Commission adopted regulation 18703 in 1976, Harry Snyder of Consumers
Union filed suit challenging the regulation. He argued that the Act was intended to prevent
industry members ofboards from making decisions that affected them financially. The
California Court ofAppeal upheld regulation 18703, stating that it did not believe the Act should
“be read to nullify the many state and local laws establishing regulatory boards and commissions
whose members are drawn from the very industry, trade or profession regulated.” (Consumers
Union of US., Inc. v. California Milk Producers Advisory Bd. (1978) 82 Cal. App.3d 433, 444,
147 CaLRptr. 265.)
Regulation 18703, subdivision (d), gave the Legislature and local lawmaking bodies a
deadline of January 1, 1979, to examine each board to determine if continued industry
representation was consistent with the public interest. If so, the legislative body had to adopt an
express finding or declaration that industry representation on a board serves the public generally.
Model language for such a finding was set forth in regulation 18703:
“... The Legislature [or other authority] declares that the
individual[s] appointed to the office of_______ is [are] intended to
Regulation 18703 in effect in 1978 stated:
“A material fmancial effect of a governmental decision on an official’s interests, as described in
Government Code Section 87 103(a) through (d), is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally unless the
decision will affect the official’s interest in substantially the same manner as it will affect all members ofthe public
or a significant segment of the public. Except as provided herein, an industry, trade or profession does not
constitute a significant segment of the general public.
(c) An industry, trade or profession constitutes a significant segment of the public if the statute, ordinance
or other provision of law which creates or authorizes the creation of the official’s agency or office contains a fmding
and declaration, including an express reference to Section 87103 of the Government Code, to the following effect:
“The Legislature [or other authority] declares that the individual[s] appointed to the office of________ is
[are] intended to represent and further the interest ofthe [specified industry, trade or profession], and that such
representation and furtherance will ultimately serve the public interest. Accordingly, the Legislature [or other
authority] finds that for purposes ofpersons who hold such office the [specified industry, trade or profession] is
tantamount to and constitutes the public generally within the meaning of Section 87103 of the Government Code.
(d) Through January 1, 1979, an industry, trade or profession constitutes a significant segment of the public
if the statute, ordinance or other provision of the law which creates or authorizes the creation of the official’s agency
contains a requirement or express authorization that members of that industry, trade, or profession hold such office.
After January 1, 1979, in the absence of an express finding and declaration ofthe type described in Paragraph (c) of
this section, such an industry, trade or profession constitutes a significant segment of the public generally only if
such a finding and declaration is implicit, taking into account the language of the statute, ordinance or other
provision of law creating or authorizing the creation of the agency, the nature and purposes of the program, any
applicable legislative history, and any other relevant circumstance.”


File No. A-99-019(a)
Page 9
represent and further the interest of the [specified industry, trade or
profession), and that such representation and furtherance will
ultimately serve the public interest. Accordingly, the Legislature
[or other authority] finds that for purposes ofpersons who hold
such office the [specified industry, trade or profession] is
tantamount to and constitutes the public generally within the
meaning of Section 87103 ofthe Government Code.” (Regulation
18703(c) in effect in 1978.)
To allow the continued participation of industry representatives on state regulatory
boards, the Legislature responded by passing over 21 statutes containing this language by 1978.
The California Horse Racing Board supervises and regulates horse racing meets throughout
California. In 1979, the Legislature adopted a finding with respect to Horse Racing Board
members, mirroring the language of regulation 18703:
“ 19424.5. Participation of horsemen; legislative intent
In order to permit the full participation ofhorsemen who may be
appointed to the board, the Legislature declares that the
appointment of such persons is intended to represent and further
the interests ofhorse owners and breeders pursuant to Section
19401, and that such representation and furtherance will ultimately
serve the public interest. Accordingly the Legislature finds race
horse owners and breeders are tantamount to and constitute the
public generally within the meaning of Section 87103 of the
Government Code.” (Business and Professions Code Section
19424.5.)
In your request for reconsideration, you questioned why a local council member such as
Mr. Roncelli would be disqualified from participating in a decision because he had received
income from a race track, when the regulators ofthe horse racing industry itself are not
disqualified because they own horses and receive purses. The answer is that in the case of the
California Horse Racing Board and other state regulatory boards, the Legislature has made a
policy judgment to permit industry representatives to continue to participate on these boards
despite their financial conflict of interest; whereas in the case of a council member, the Act’s
conflict of interest rules govern.
c. Freedom of Speech and Policy Arguments. The goal ofthe Act’s conflicts laws is
unbiased decision-making. In its findings and purposes, the Act states:
‘
Consumers Union, supra, 82 Cal.App.3d 433 at 438.


File No. A-99-019(a)
Page 10
“(b) Public officials, whether elected or appointed, should
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests ofpersons
who have supported them ....“ (Section 81001(b).)
“(c) Assets and income ofpublic officials which may be
materially affected by their official actions should be disclosed and
in appropriate circumstances the officials should be disqualified
from acting in order that conflicts of interest may be avoided.”
(Section 81002(c).)
In considering whether an elected official should be disqualified from a decision, the
desire for unbiased decision-making must be weighed against the democratic interest in
permitting the official elected by the voters to act. But courts have found that the Act’s conflict-
of-interest and financial disclosure laws strike an appropriate balance. The Court of Appeal
stated in Consumers Union that: “The act is not so overly broad as to destroy participatory
democracy by those with limited, immaterial financial interests in their official actions, yet in
certain circumstances officials are disqualified from serving in order to.avoid conflicts of
interests.” (82 Cal.App.3d at 443.)
The Act’s provisions dealing with the disclosure of economic interests by public officials
were upheld by the California Supreme Court in Hays v. Wood (1979) 25 CaL3d 772, 160
Cal.Rptr. 102, against challenges that the required disclosure was unconstitutionally overbroad
and a violation of officials’ privacy rights. The court held that the Act’s disclosure scheme was
not overbroad and that any infringements on the official’s right to privacy or associational
freedom was justified by the limited disclosure needed to prevent conflicts of interests.
In County ofNevada v. MacMillen (1974) 11 Cal.3d 662, 114 Cal.Rptr. 345, the
California Supreme Court upheld a 1973 conflict-of-interest statute that was a predecessor to the
Act. The 1973 statute required financial disclosure by high-level elected officials and prohibited
them from participating in governmental decisions about matters in which they had a financial
interest. In upholding the conflict-of-interest law in Nevada, the Supreme Court expressed its
view that:
“... neither the right to privacy, nor the right to seek and hold
public office, must inevitably prevail over the right of the public to
an honest and impartial government.” Id. at 672.
In this situation, the basic policy underlying the Act is not offended by, but rather
supports requiring council member Roncelli’s disqualification from a decision that will affect an
entity that is a source of income to him.


File No. A-99-019(a)
Page 11
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
p.
By:
Hyla P. Wagner
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
You are requesting reconsideration of our recent advice letter to you, No. A-99-019. That
letter concluded that if council member Roncelli has received over $250 in income from the Los
Angeles Turf Club in the past 12 months, from owning horses that race at Santa Anita, he must
disqualify himself from participating in decisions about the development ofportions of Santa
Anita Park that would have a material financial effect on the Los Angeles Turf Club, or on any
business entity that is a parent, subsidiary, or is “otherwise related” to the Turf Club.
a. You reiterate your argument that the Los Angeles Turf Club is not a source of income
to council member Roncelli.
b. You argue that state law precludes the application of conflict of interest provisions to
race horse owners, relying on Business and Professions Code section 19424.5, which provides
that members of the California Horse Racing Board are not disqualified from making decisions
about the horse racing industry even though they have received income from horse racing.(a)
c. You raise First Amendment and policy arguments as to why the Act’s conflicts of
interest laws should not prohibit Mr. Roncelli from participating in decisions about the
development ofproperty at the Santa Anita race track.
sections.conclusion:
a. Despite the additional information and arguments you set forth, we stand by the
conclusion in our original advice letter that, for purposes ofthe Act, the Los Angeles Turf Club
is considered a source of income to council member Roncelli. We recognize that state law
governs horse racing and prescribes the method for determining the amounts paid out of the
purse to winning race horse owners, as you have described in your requests for advice. For
purposes of the Act’s conflict of interest laws, however, we do not find any precedence or
rationale for ignoring the Los Angeles Turf Club as a legal entity, and looking through it to find
that the individual bettors are sources of income to council member Roncelli instead ofthe
corporation.
b. Regulation 18707.4 of the Political Reform Act permits a narrow exception to the
conflict ofinterest rules for industry representatives on regulatory boards. Business and
Professions Code section 19424.5 was adopted pursuant to this regulation, and only applies to
members ofthe California Horse Racing Board. It does not apply to local council members like
Mr. Roncelli.
c. The financial disclosure and conflict ofinterest rules ofthe Political Reform Act have
been upheld in numerous cases. The Act’s policy goals of unbiased decision-making support
council member Ronceffi’s disqualification from a decision affecting an entity that is a source of
income to him.

determined by established rate schedules negotiated in advance of a racing meet by the
Thoroughbred Owners of California and the Turf Club. Pursuant to state law, race tracks in
California are accorded a slot ofthe calendar to conduct competition at their location. The Turf
Club operates Santa Anita Park for this purpose during the December - April time period. This
time slot is also referred to as the racing meet.
The actual purses to be paid for any one race, or for the day’s races, initially are
determined by the racing secretary, an employee of the Turf Club, based on projections of the
handle (amount wagered). The purse is often revised via negotiations between the Thoroughbred
Owners of California and the Turf Club during the course of the race meeting based on the actual
handle. The handle which is the amount bet, comes from both on track betting and off-track
betting.


(a)
The amount of the total purse is determined through negotiations between the TOC and
the Turf Club prior to the race meet. Usually, revenue is paid to those owners whose horses
finish one through six in any given race. This revenue is a percentage established by statute. Of
course, the races are competitive and the results are a matter of speculation.
In sum, a race horse owner may derive revenue ifhis or her horse finishes within a
required place in a race. If the owner is entitled to revenues, their source is the handle which is
the money bet by members of the public who attend the racing event at Santa Anita Park and at
off-track betting facilities. The amount of the purse and its percentage allocation is pre
determined before the racing meet based on the prospective handle. Similarly, the handle is the
source of income to the Turf Club.
sections.analysis:
a. Source of Income. The analysis with respect to whether the Los Angeles Turf Club is
considered a source of income to council member Roncelli under the Act is set forth in our initial
advice letter to you, No. A-99-019, and summarized here.
The purpose of the Act’s conflict of interest provisions is to ensure that government
officials make decisions unbiased by their own financial interests. (Section 8 1001(b).) In this
case, the decisions facing Mr. Roncelli are upcoming Arcadia City Council decisions about
Magna’s proposal to develop part ofthe parking lot at Santa Anita Park into theaters, restaurants,
and other commercial and residential uses.
Section 87100 ofthe Act prohibits a public official, such as council member Roncelli,
from making a governmental decision in which he has afinancial interest. Section 87103 ofthe
Act states that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official or on any ofthe official’s economic interests.
Section 87103 sets forth the official’s economic interests in five categories: (a) a
business entity in which the official has an investment of $1,000 or more; (b) real property in
which the official has an investment of $1,000 or more; (c) any individual, business or entity that
has been a source ofincome to the official of $250 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision; (d) a business entity that the official manages or is an employee of; and (e) an
individual or entity that has made gifts to the official of $250 or more within 12 months prior to
the decision. (Section 87103 (a)-(e).)
In council member Roncelli’s case, the economic interest at issue is a source of income to
him. Section 87103(c) states that an official’s economic interests include:


(a)
“Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course ofbusiness on
terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.”
In this case, council member Roncelli has received over $250 from the Los Angeles Turf
Club in the past. He also has an ownership interest in some horses that may, within 12 months of
upcoming city council decisions, derive revenue from racing at Santa Anita Park equal to or in
excess of $250. As discussed in the previous advice letter, it is not disputed that when Mr.
Roncelli receives prize money from a racing meet, those funds are accurately characterized as
income under section 82030. However, you argue that the source of income to Mr. Roncelli is
not the Los Angeles Turf Club, but rather the individual bettors.
In our initial advice letter, we concluded that for purposes ofthe Act, the Los Angeles
Turf Club is considered the source of income to council member Roncelli. The analysis
supporting this conclusion is fully set forth at pages 5-7 of that letter. In summary, we found that
despite the fact that the funds the Turf Club distributes come from the money bet by the public,
the Turf Club is the corporate entity that is the source of income to Mr. Roncelli.
The question of whether the Turf Club is a source of income to council member Roncelli
is rather straightforward. The checks Mr. Roncelli receives from racing horses at Santa Anita are
issued by the Los Angeles Turf Club. Mr. Roncelli reports income from the Los Angeles Turf
Club, not from individual bettors, on his income tax returns. And the entity that is legally liable
for paying owners ofwinning horses at Santa Anita isthe Los Angeles Turf Club, not the
individual bettors. It is the Los Angeles Turf Club to whom Mr. Roncelli would have legal
recourse if they failed to pay him for a winning race or paid him the wrong amount. We
recognize that the source ofthe funds the Los Angeles Turf Club distributes comes from the
betting public and that the method for distributing funds to horse owners is prescribed by statute,
which requires that the Turf Club and the Thoroughbred Owners of California negotiate the
amount ofthe purse paid to winning race horse owners. However, just because an official
receives a payment from a corporation, at a rate or amount that is set by statute, does not
necessarily mean that the corporation making the payment is not considered a source of income
to the official under the Act.
As we discussed in our previous advice letter, in applying the Act’s conflicts rules, the
norm is to respect the corporate form. It is only in unusual circumstances where the Commission
or previous advice has “pierced” through entities, such as corporations, which are the apparent
source of income, to treat the controlling person as an additional source of income or economic
interest of the official’s. (See e.g., Lahr Advice Letter, No. 1-98-298; Brown Advice Letter, A-
97-122; Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-80-03-069; In re Nord (1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6). In such
cases, the advice looked through an entity to find that an additional individual or entity should


File NoA-99-019(a)
be considered an economic interest of the officials.
In this case, you argue for looking through
the Los Angeles Turf Club to find, in effect, no economic interest ofthe council member’s,
because no individual bettor will be a source of $250 or more in income to him.
The Los Angeles Turf Club is more than an “intermediary” or “pass-through” for funds.
As stated in the facts you provided, the purses for race meetings, i.e., what is paid to winning
horse owners, are determined by rate schedules negotiated in advance of a racing meet by the
Thoroughbred Owners of California and the Los Angeles Turf Club. So the Turf Club is an
active participant in negotiating the amount that will be paid to winning horse owners.
In your request for reconsideration, you make the additional argument that “house
gambling” is illegal in California and that by “converting Santa Anita into a source of income
with regard to monies bet by thó public and paid out by statute to horse owners via the purse, the
FPPC is, in effect, analogizing Santa Anita to the ‘house’ with regard to gambling....” We do not
agree that finding the Los Angeles Turf Club as the source of income to council member
Roncelli for purposes of the Act’s conflict of interest laws will convert the system of legalized
gambling in California into a system ofhouse gambling, as you argued. The Zweben Advice
Letter, No. 1-94-3 80, concluded that a local official could not participate in the cardroom
approval process ifhe had received $250 or more in horse race winnings from the racetrack that
was requesting a license for a cardroom gaming facility. The Zweben advice letter is consistent
with our conclusion that the Los Angeles Turf Club is a source of income to council member
Roncelli, and has had no effect on the status of gambling in California.
b. Industry Representatives on Regulatory Boards. Next you argue that because
members of the California Horse Racing Board are permitted to participate in decisions affecting
the industry even though they own race horses and receive purses, council member Roncelli
should be able to as well. Shortly after the Political Reform Act was adopted in 1974, the
question arose whether the Act’s conflict of interest rules should prohibit industry members of
state regulatory boards from making decisions affecting the regulated industry. In 1976, the
Commission adopted regulation 18703, the predecessor of current regulation 18707.4 (previously
numbered 18703.3), in order to interpret the Political Reform Act harmoniously with the
numerous state statutes providing for industry representation on regulatory boards. Regulation
18707.4 creates a narrow exception from disqualification under the Act’s conflict of interest laws
for members ofboards or commissions who, by law, are required to have a particular economic
interest to be on the board. Regulation 18707.4 provides:
“18707.4. Public Generally: Appointed Members of Boards
and Commissions
(a) For the purposes of Government Code section 87103, the
‘public generally’ exception applies to appointed members of
boards and commissions who are appointed to represent a specific
economic interest, as specified in section 87103(a) through (d), if
all of the following apply:


(a)
(1) The statute, ordinance, or other provision of law which
creates or authorizes the creation of the board or commission
contains a finding and declaration that the persons appointed to the
board or commission are appointed to represent and further the
interests of the specific economic interest.
(2) The member is required to have the economic interest the
member represents.
(3) The board’s or commission’s decision does not have a
material financial effect on any other economic interest held by the
member, other than the economic interest the member was
appointed to represent.
(4) The decision ofthe board or commission will financially
affect the member’s economic interest in a manner that is
substantially the same or proportionately the same as the decision
will financially affect a significant segment of the persons the
member was appointed to represent.
(b) In the absence of an express finding and declaration of the
type described in Title 2, California Code ofRegulations, section
18707.4(b)(l), the ‘public generally’ exception only applies if such
a finding and declaration is implicit, taking into account the
language ofthe statute, ordinance, or other provision of law
creating or authorizing the creation ofthe board or commission, the
nature and purposes ofthe program, any applicable legislative
history, and any other relevant circumstance.”
The Commission discussed the history ofthe exception for industry representatives on
regulatory boards in the Callanan Opinion:
“The subsections were adopted in an effort to reconcile the
conflict ofinterest provisions ofthe Political Reform Act with
other statutes which require certain boards to include as members
persons who represent the industry, trade or profession which the
board oversees. The Commission was persuaded that when the
legislative body which creates a regulatory board determines that
industry views and expertise should be represented on the board,
the Political Reform Act should not be interpreted to prevent
industry members from participating in board decisions affecting
the industry.” (In re Callanan, 4 FPPC Ops. 33 (1978), p. 4.)
The Callanan Opinion dealt with the State Board ofFuneral Directors and Embalmers, an
eight-member board, ofwhom three members were required to be licensed funeral directors or
embalmers, and the remaining five were required to be public members. The Commission


(a)
applied regulation l8703, the predecessor to current regulation 18707.4. The Commission
concluded that the three industry members ofthe Funeral Board were not required to disqualify
themselves from a decision about requiring the consent ofnext ofkin before embalming a body,
which would have required many in the industry to purchase refrigeration equipment.
After the Commission adopted regulation 18703 in 1976, Harry Snyder of Consumers
Union filed suit challenging the regulation. He argued that the Act was intended to prevent
industry members ofboards from making decisions that affected them financially. The
California Court ofAppeal upheld regulation 18703, stating that it did not believe the Act should
“be read to nullify the many state and local laws establishing regulatory boards and commissions
whose members are drawn from the very industry, trade or profession regulated.” (Consumers
Union of US., Inc. v. California Milk Producers Advisory Bd. (1978) 82 Cal. App.3d 433, 444,
147 CaLRptr. 265.)
Regulation 18703, subdivision (d), gave the Legislature and local lawmaking bodies a
deadline of January 1, 1979, to examine each board to determine if continued industry
representation was consistent with the public interest. If so, the legislative body had to adopt an
express finding or declaration that industry representation on a board serves the public generally.
Model language for such a finding was set forth in regulation 18703:
“... The Legislature [or other authority] declares that the
individual[s] appointed to the office of_______ is [are] intended to
Regulation 18703 in effect in 1978 stated:
“A material fmancial effect of a governmental decision on an official’s interests, as described in
Government Code Section 87 103(a) through (d), is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally unless the
decision will affect the official’s interest in substantially the same manner as it will affect all members ofthe public
or a significant segment of the public. Except as provided herein, an industry, trade or profession does not
constitute a significant segment of the general public.
(c) An industry, trade or profession constitutes a significant segment of the public if the statute, ordinance
or other provision of law which creates or authorizes the creation of the official’s agency or office contains a fmding
and declaration, including an express reference to Section 87103 of the Government Code, to the following effect:
“The Legislature [or other authority] declares that the individual[s] appointed to the office of________ is
[are] intended to represent and further the interest ofthe [specified industry, trade or profession], and that such
representation and furtherance will ultimately serve the public interest. Accordingly, the Legislature [or other
authority] finds that for purposes ofpersons who hold such office the [specified industry, trade or profession] is
tantamount to and constitutes the public generally within the meaning of Section 87103 of the Government Code.
(d) Through January 1, 1979, an industry, trade or profession constitutes a significant segment of the public
if the statute, ordinance or other provision of the law which creates or authorizes the creation of the official’s agency
contains a requirement or express authorization that members of that industry, trade, or profession hold such office.
After January 1, 1979, in the absence of an express finding and declaration ofthe type described in Paragraph (c) of
this section, such an industry, trade or profession constitutes a significant segment of the public generally only if
such a finding and declaration is implicit, taking into account the language of the statute, ordinance or other
provision of law creating or authorizing the creation of the agency, the nature and purposes of the program, any
applicable legislative history, and any other relevant circumstance.”


(a)
represent and further the interest of the [specified industry, trade or
profession), and that such representation and furtherance will
ultimately serve the public interest. Accordingly, the Legislature
[or other authority] finds that for purposes ofpersons who hold
such office the [specified industry, trade or profession] is
tantamount to and constitutes the public generally within the
meaning of Section 87103 ofthe Government Code.” (Regulation
18703(c) in effect in 1978.)
To allow the continued participation of industry representatives on state regulatory
boards, the Legislature responded by passing over 21 statutes containing this language by 1978.
The California Horse Racing Board supervises and regulates horse racing meets throughout
California. In 1979, the Legislature adopted a finding with respect to Horse Racing Board
members, mirroring the language of regulation 18703:
“ 19424.5. Participation of horsemen; legislative intent
In order to permit the full participation ofhorsemen who may be
appointed to the board, the Legislature declares that the
appointment of such persons is intended to represent and further
the interests ofhorse owners and breeders pursuant to Section
19401, and that such representation and furtherance will ultimately
serve the public interest. Accordingly the Legislature finds race
horse owners and breeders are tantamount to and constitute the
public generally within the meaning of Section 87103 of the
Government Code.” (Business and Professions Code Section
19424.5.)
In your request for reconsideration, you questioned why a local council member such as
Mr. Roncelli would be disqualified from participating in a decision because he had received
income from a race track, when the regulators ofthe horse racing industry itself are not
disqualified because they own horses and receive purses. The answer is that in the case of the
California Horse Racing Board and other state regulatory boards, the Legislature has made a
policy judgment to permit industry representatives to continue to participate on these boards
despite their financial conflict of interest; whereas in the case of a council member, the Act’s
conflict of interest rules govern.
c. Freedom of Speech and Policy Arguments. The goal ofthe Act’s conflicts laws is
unbiased decision-making. In its findings and purposes, the Act states:
‘
Consumers Union, supra, 82 Cal.App.3d 433 at 438.


(a)
“(b) Public officials, whether elected or appointed, should
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests ofpersons
who have supported them ....“ (Section 81001(b).)
“(c) Assets and income ofpublic officials which may be
materially affected by their official actions should be disclosed and
in appropriate circumstances the officials should be disqualified
from acting in order that conflicts of interest may be avoided.”
(Section 81002(c).)
In considering whether an elected official should be disqualified from a decision, the
desire for unbiased decision-making must be weighed against the democratic interest in
permitting the official elected by the voters to act. But courts have found that the Act’s conflict-
of-interest and financial disclosure laws strike an appropriate balance. The Court of Appeal
stated in Consumers Union that: “The act is not so overly broad as to destroy participatory
democracy by those with limited, immaterial financial interests in their official actions, yet in
certain circumstances officials are disqualified from serving in order to.avoid conflicts of
interests.” (82 Cal.App.3d at 443.)
The Act’s provisions dealing with the disclosure of economic interests by public officials
were upheld by the California Supreme Court in Hays v. Wood (1979) 25 CaL3d 772, 160
Cal.Rptr. 102, against challenges that the required disclosure was unconstitutionally overbroad
and a violation of officials’ privacy rights. The court held that the Act’s disclosure scheme was
not overbroad and that any infringements on the official’s right to privacy or associational
freedom was justified by the limited disclosure needed to prevent conflicts of interests.
In County ofNevada v. MacMillen (1974) 11 Cal.3d 662, 114 Cal.Rptr. 345, the
California Supreme Court upheld a 1973 conflict-of-interest statute that was a predecessor to the
Act. The 1973 statute required financial disclosure by high-level elected officials and prohibited
them from participating in governmental decisions about matters in which they had a financial
interest. In upholding the conflict-of-interest law in Nevada, the Supreme Court expressed its
view that:
“... neither the right to privacy, nor the right to seek and hold
public office, must inevitably prevail over the right of the public to
an honest and impartial government.” Id. at 672.
In this situation, the basic policy underlying the Act is not offended by, but rather
supports requiring council member Roncelli’s disqualification from a decision that will affect an
entity that is a source of income to him.


(a)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You are requesting reconsideration of our recent advice letter to you, No. A-99-019. That
letter concluded that if council member Roncelli has received over $250 in income from the Los
Angeles Turf Club in the past 12 months, from owning horses that race at Santa Anita, he must
disqualify himself from participating in decisions about the development ofportions of Santa
Anita Park that would have a material financial effect on the Los Angeles Turf Club, or on any
business entity that is a parent, subsidiary, or is “otherwise related” to the Turf Club.
a. You reiterate your argument that the Los Angeles Turf Club is not a source of income
to council member Roncelli.
b. You argue that state law precludes the application of conflict of interest provisions to
race horse owners, relying on Business and Professions Code section 19424.5, which provides
that members of the California Horse Racing Board are not disqualified from making decisions
about the horse racing industry even though they have received income from horse racing.(a)
c. You raise First Amendment and policy arguments as to why the Act’s conflicts of
interest laws should not prohibit Mr. Roncelli from participating in decisions about the
development ofproperty at the Santa Anita race track.

CONCLUSION: a. Despite the additional information and arguments you set forth, we stand by the
conclusion in our original advice letter that, for purposes ofthe Act, the Los Angeles Turf Club
is considered a source of income to council member Roncelli. We recognize that state law
governs horse racing and prescribes the method for determining the amounts paid out of the
purse to winning race horse owners, as you have described in your requests for advice. For
purposes of the Act’s conflict of interest laws, however, we do not find any precedence or
rationale for ignoring the Los Angeles Turf Club as a legal entity, and looking through it to find
that the individual bettors are sources of income to council member Roncelli instead ofthe
corporation.
b. Regulation 18707.4 of the Political Reform Act permits a narrow exception to the
conflict ofinterest rules for industry representatives on regulatory boards. Business and
Professions Code section 19424.5 was adopted pursuant to this regulation, and only applies to
members ofthe California Horse Racing Board. It does not apply to local council members like
Mr. Roncelli.
c. The financial disclosure and conflict ofinterest rules ofthe Political Reform Act have
been upheld in numerous cases. The Act’s policy goals of unbiased decision-making support
council member Ronceffi’s disqualification from a decision affecting an entity that is a source of
income to him.

That letter concluded that if council member Roncelli has received over $250 in income from the Los Angeles Turf Club in the past 12 months, from owning horses that race at Santa Anita, he must disqualify himself from participating in decisions about the development ofportions of Santa Anita Park that would have a material financial effect on the Los Angeles Turf Club, or on any business entity that is a parent, subsidiary, or is “otherwise related” to the Turf Club. a. You reiterate your argument that the Los Angeles Turf Club is not a source of income to council member Roncelli. b. You argue that state law precludes the application of conflict of interest provisions to race horse owners, relying on Business and Professions Code section 19424.5, which provides that members of the California Horse Racing Board are not disqualified from making decisions about the horse racing industry even though they have received income from horse racing. Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-99-019(a) Page 2 c. You raise First Amendment and policy arguments as to why the Act’s conflicts of interest laws should not prohibit Mr. Roncelli from participating in decisions about the development ofproperty at the Santa Anita race track. CONCLUSIONS a. Despite the additional information and arguments you set forth, we stand by the conclusion in our original advice letter that, for purposes ofthe Act, the Los Angeles Turf Club is considered a source of income to council member Roncelli. We recognize that state law governs horse racing and prescribes the method for determining the amounts paid out of the purse to winning race horse owners, as you have described in your requests for advice. For purposes of the Act’s conflict of interest laws, however, we do not find any precedence or rationale for ignoring the Los Angeles Turf Club as a legal entity, and looking through it to find that the individual bettors are sources of income to council member Roncelli instead ofthe corporation. b. Regulation 18707.4 of the Political Reform Act permits a narrow exception to the conflict ofinterest rules for industry representatives on regulatory boards. Business and Professions Code section 19424.5 was adopted pursuant to this regulation, and only applies to members ofthe California Horse Racing Board. It does not apply to local council members like Mr.
